title: Moonshot AI releases Kimi K3, a 2.8T open-weight MoE with a million-token context
subtitle: The Alibaba-backed Beijing lab shipped the largest open-source model to date — a Mixture-of-Experts stack built on Kimi Delta Attention and Attention Residuals, aimed at agent workloads and long-horizon coding.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Moonshot AI, the Beijing lab backed by Alibaba, released Kimi K3 on July 16 as a 2.8-trillion-parameter open-weight Mixture-of-Experts model with a 1-million-token context window. Two variants shipped at launch — K3 Max for chat and agent workloads, and K3 Swarm Max for large-scale parallel processing — and both are available on Kimi Code and inside the Kimi app under a Modified MIT license. Moonshot calls it the first open 3T-class model and reports up to 6.3x faster decoding in million-token contexts against comparable dense baselines.

The architecture is what makes the size claim more than a headline number. Kimi K3 is built on two novel ideas that Moonshot developed in the K2 line and hardened for K3: Kimi Delta Attention, a linear-attention variant that keeps memory pressure sub-quadratic across long contexts, and Attention Residuals, which route information around attention layers to preserve gradient flow at depth. Together they let the model activate only 16 of 896 experts per token while still holding coherent state across a million-token window — a combination that closes a real gap with proprietary systems from Anthropic and OpenAI on long-horizon coding and agent benchmarks.

The release is the most direct signal yet that the open-weight frontier is a two-country race. Thinking Machines' Inkling landed a day earlier at 975B, Meituan's LongCat 2 and Alibaba's Qwen series have been shipping monthly, and Zhipu's founder used an internal memo two weekends ago to argue openness itself is a strategic posture. Kimi K3 is now the largest open model on any leaderboard, and it arrived from a lab whose primary funder is Alibaba — the same firm that had banned Claude Code inside its own walls a week earlier. The signal is that Chinese labs are betting scale plus open weights beats access to closed U.S. APIs.

A note for learners: 'open-weight' does not mean 'runnable at home.' A 2.8T MoE model — even one that activates a small fraction of experts per token — still needs a serious cluster to serve, and quantized single-workstation runs will trade quality for feasibility. The value of the release for a student or early-career engineer is in the paper and the reference code: Kimi Delta Attention and Attention Residuals are ideas you can read, reimplement at a small scale, and understand end-to-end. That is the education that closed frontier labs cannot give you.
